critical_bugs.title: FragmentProgram shader objects never disposed - memory leak
critical_bugs.description:
The _program field stores a ui.FragmentProgram but is never explicitly disposed.
    FragmentProgram objects hold GPU resources (compiled shader programs) that must be
    manually released. When switching fractals or hot-reloading, old shader programs
    accumulate in GPU memory.
evidence: Line 133: ui.FragmentProgram? _program; (field declaration)
Line 254: _program = program; (assignment on shader load)
Lines 354-358: dispose() method does NOT dispose _program
No _program?.dispose() call anywhere in the class
critical_bugs.impact: GPU memory leak. After switching between 8 fractals multiple times, GPU memory exhaustion possible.
critical_bugs.fix: Add _program?.dispose() in dispose() method before super.dispose()

critical_bugs.title: No size validation in FractalCanvas.paint() - NaN/crash risk
critical_bugs.description:
FractalCanvas.paint() directly uses size.width and size.height without validation.
    If the widget is laid out with Size.zero or during rapid layout changes, this can:
    1. Pass 0.0 to shader uniforms causing division by zero in shader
    2. Create Rect.fromLTWH(0, 0, 0, 0) which may cause rendering artifacts
    3. Crash on some GPU drivers that don't handle zero-sized draw calls
evidence: Line 21: shader = program.fragmentShader() (creates new shader instance)
Line 22: module.setUniforms(shader, state, size, time) (size passed unchecked)
Line 27-29: canvas.drawRect(Rect.fromLTWH(0, 0, size.width, size.height), paint)
No if (size.width <= 0 || size.height <= 0) guard
critical_bugs.impact: Widget layout edge case crashes. Shader division by zero. Black screen or GPU driver crash.
critical_bugs.fix: Add early return if size.width <= 0 || size.height <= 0 at start of paint()

high_priority_bugs.title: New FragmentShader instance created every frame - performance degradation
high_priority_bugs.description:
FractalCanvas.paint() calls program.fragmentShader() on EVERY frame (60 FPS).
    This creates a new shader instance, allocates GPU resources, and potentially
    recompiles or re-links the shader program each frame. Should create shader
    once and reuse, only setting uniforms per frame.
evidence: Line 20: final shader = program.fragmentShader() (inside paint(), called 60x/sec)
CustomPainter.paint() is called every frame during animation
No shader instance caching or reuse mechanism
high_priority_bugs.impact: 60 FPS → ~30 FPS on mid-range devices. Increased CPU/GPU overhead. Stuttering during animations.
high_priority_bugs.fix: Cache shader instance in CustomPainter state, create once, reuse per frame

high_priority_bugs.title: Zoom momentum unbounded - can reach NaN/Infinity
high_priority_bugs.description:
_applyZoomMomentum() multiplies zoom exponentially without bounds checking.
    Line 200: newZoom = view.zoom * (1.0 + decayedVelocity * 0.02)
    If velocity is large (fast pinch gesture), this can compound over 800ms to
    push zoom beyond 1e12 clamp limit, or with negative velocity, reach 0.0 or NaN.
    updateZoom() clamps to [1e-9, 1e12] but momentum can still overshoot.
evidence: Line 200: newZoom = view.zoom * (1.0 + decayedVelocity * 0.02)
Line 294: updateZoom clamps to [1e-9, 1e12]
Line 465: _zoomVelocity = (scaleChange - 1.0) * 10.0 (can be large)
No velocity magnitude limiting before momentum application
high_priority_bugs.impact: Extreme zoom values cause precision loss, black screen, or shader artifacts. NaN propagates to shader uniforms.
high_priority_bugs.fix: Clamp decayedVelocity magnitude before applying. Add sanity check in _applyZoomMomentum().

high_priority_bugs.title: Shader load race condition - multiple concurrent loads possible
high_priority_bugs.description:
_loadShader() checks _loading flag at start, but setState runs asynchronously.
    If user rapidly switches fractals, multiple _loadShader() calls can run
    concurrently because:
    1. Line 241: if (_loading) return; (checked)
    2. Line 244: _loading = true; (set)
    3. Lines 252-266: async shader load (takes 50-200ms)
    4. During load, another module switch calls _loadShader() again
    5. Line 255: if (mounted) setState() can overwrite _program from previous load
    
    Result: Wrong shader assigned to wrong module, rendering incorrect fractal.
evidence: Line 240-305: _loadShader is async
Line 241: Early return only checks _loading, not module ID
Line 743: if (_shaderAsset != module.shaderAsset && !_loading) (race window)
No atomic operation or module ID tracking to prevent stale loads
high_priority_bugs.impact: Visual bug: wrong fractal rendered. Rare but reproducible with rapid module switching.
high_priority_bugs.fix: Add module ID tracking. Compare module ID before setState() to reject stale loads.

medium_priority_bugs.title: CPU fallback only supports 2D but policy applies to all modules
medium_priority_bugs.description:
Deep zoom precision policy can trigger CPU fallback for ANY module, but
    CPU renderer (CpuFractalRenderer) only supports 2D fractals with specific
    formulas (mandelbrot, julia, celtic, buffalo, burning_ship, tricorn).
    
    Line 712: if (shouldUseCpuFallback && module.dimension == FractalDimension.twoD)
    This guard prevents rendering 3D fractals on CPU (correct), but doesn't check
    if the 2D module is actually supported by CPU renderer.
    
    CPU renderer switch at line 345-364 only handles 6 formulas. Newer fractals
    like 'multibrot3', 'phoenix', 'nova' will fall through to default case which
    uses standard Mandelbrot formula - WRONG OUTPUT.
evidence: Line 707: shouldUseCpuFallback = _precisionPolicy.shouldUseCpuFallback()
Line 712: Checks dimension == twoD but not module.id
cpu_fractal_renderer.dart line 345-364: switch only handles 6 module IDs
DeepZoomPrecisionPolicy has 8 modules but CPU only supports 6
medium_priority_bugs.impact: Deep zoom on unsupported fractals shows WRONG formula. Silent correctness bug.
medium_priority_bugs.fix: Add CPU-supported module whitelist check before CPU fallback, or extend CPU renderer to support all 8 formulas.

medium_priority_bugs.title: Gesture focal point math uses unclamped zoom - potential NaN
medium_priority_bugs.description:
During pinch-zoom gestures, focal point calculation divides by zoom.
    
    Line 418: final dxWorld = (rawDx * cosR + rawDy * sinR) / math.max(1e-9, _startZoom);
    
    This uses _startZoom which is captured at gesture start (line 374).
    If zoom becomes 0 or very small (1e-15) through momentum or direct manipulation,
    math.max(1e-9, _startZoom) prevents division by zero, but...
    
    Line 453-458: Focal point calculation for pinch zoom uses worldX/worldY:
      final worldX = startN.dx / _startZoom + _startPan.x;
    
    This does NOT have the 1e-9 guard, so if _startZoom is 0.0, this creates NaN.
    NaN propagates through Vector2 operations and into shader uniforms.
evidence: Line 374: _startZoom = controller.view.zoom; (captured, could be ~0)
Line 418: Uses math.max(1e-9, _startZoom) - SAFE
Line 453: final worldX = startN.dx / _startZoom + _startPan.x; - UNSAFE
updateZoom clamps to 1e-9 but gesture can start before clamp applied
medium_priority_bugs.impact: NaN propagates to pan coordinates, shader receives NaN uniforms, screen goes black or shows artifacts.

medium_priority_bugs.title: Animation controller state inconsistency in test mode
medium_priority_bugs.description:
build() method manages animation controller state dynamically:
    - If animationEnabled=true and not animating, calls repeat()
    - If animationEnabled=false and is animating, calls stop()
    
    But line 184: if (!_isTest) { _animationController.repeat(); }
    In test mode, animation is never started in initState(), which is correct.
    
    However, if animationEnabled toggles from false→true in test mode, the
    build() logic at line 642 tries to call repeat() even in test mode.
    
    This causes inconsistent state where test expects frozen time but animation
    runs anyway if animationEnabled prop changes after widget creation.
evidence: Line 184: if (!_isTest) _animationController.repeat() (initState)
Line 642: if (!_isTest && !_animationController.isAnimating) (build)
Line 646-651: Stops animation if enabled=false, regardless of test mode
Test mode relies on animation never running, but dynamic enabling breaks this
medium_priority_bugs.impact: Widget tests fail with 'pumpAndSettle timeout' if animationEnabled changes during test. Rare.
medium_priority_bugs.fix: Wrap all animation state changes in if (!_isTest) guards, not just initState.

low_priority_bugs.title: Minimap tap-to-pan ignores fractal rotation - inaccurate navigation
low_priority_bugs.description:
_handleTap() converts tap position to fractal coordinates but doesn't
    account for view.rotation.z which affects 2D fractal orientation.
    
    When user rotates a 2D fractal and then taps the minimap, the pan
    calculation uses unrotated coordinates, causing the view to jump to
    the wrong location.
    
    Minimap viewport rectangle (_ViewportPainter) also doesn't draw rotated,
    so visual feedback doesn't match actual viewport orientation.
evidence: Line 88-93: Tap converts to normalizedX/Y but no rotation applied
Line 93: newPan = Vector2(normalizedX * scale, normalizedY * scale)
No rotation matrix multiplication
_ViewportPainter doesn't apply rotation transform to rect
low_priority_bugs.impact: Minimap navigation inaccurate when fractal is rotated. Confusing UX but not a crash.
low_priority_bugs.fix: Apply inverse rotation matrix to tap coordinates before setting pan.

low_priority_bugs.title: CPU renderer doesn't dispose old ui.Image when re-rendering
low_priority_bugs.description:
_CpuFractalRendererState stores rendered image in _image field.
    When re-rendering (zoom/pan changes), setState() overwrites _image
    with a new ui.Image without disposing the old one.
    
    ui.Image objects hold GPU texture memory and should be disposed when
    no longer needed. During continuous pan/zoom, this creates a minor
    memory leak of old image textures.
evidence: Line 39: ui.Image? _image; (field declaration)
Line 134: setState(() { _image = img; ... }) (overwrites without dispose)
Line 63-66: dispose() doesn't dispose _image
Each render creates new ui.Image via decodeImageFromPixels
low_priority_bugs.impact: Minor memory leak during CPU fallback usage. ~5-10 MB over extended deep zoom session.
low_priority_bugs.fix: Add _image?.dispose() before assigning new image in setState().

low_priority_bugs.title: FractalCanvas.shouldRepaint uses != for Map equality - always repaints
low_priority_bugs.description:
shouldRepaint() compares oldDelegate.state != state.
    FractalRenderState is immutable class but uses default == which compares
    reference equality for params Map, not deep equality.
    
    Since FractalController creates new Map instances on every param change,
    state != oldState is ALWAYS true even if param values are identical.
    
    This causes unnecessary repaints even when nothing changed, though in
    practice this doesn't matter much because time changes every frame anyway.
evidence: Line 35: oldDelegate.state != state (reference comparison)
FractalRenderState doesn't override == operator
params is Map<String, Object> without custom equality
Every notifyListeners() creates new state even if values unchanged
low_priority_bugs.impact: Unnecessary CustomPainter repaints. Negligible performance impact because time changes anyway.
low_priority_bugs.fix: Override == in FractalRenderState for deep equality, or use Equatable package.
